inlineContent.text: KeyboardKit lets you create fully customizable
in a few lines of code, using SwiftUI. It extends Apple’s limited APIs and provides you with a lot more functionality than what is otherwise available.
extends KeyboardKit with pro features, such as fully localized keyboards and services, autocomplete, an emoji keyboard, AI supporting capabilities, themes, dictation, etc.
KeyboardKit Pro can be installed with the Swift Package Manager:
Unlike KeyboardKit, KeyboardKit Pro is a binary target and must ONLY be added to the app target. If you add it to any other target, it may crash at runtime.
The
guide helps you get started with KeyboardKit, and the
article describes the essential types in the library.
You should also read the
guide, about the very strict memory limitations that Apple puts on keyboard extensions.
KeyboardKit supports
keyboard-specific
s:
KeyboardKit Pro unlocks localized callouts, input sets, layouts & services for all supported locales, as shown in the
article.
KeyboardKit Pro extends KeyboardKit’s
with Pro features:
- KeyboardKit Pro unlocks more essential tools, keyboard previews, etc.
- KeyboardKit Pro unlocks features that are needed for AI.
- KeyboardKit Pro unlocks app-specific screens & views.
- KeyboardKit Pro unlocks on-device & remote autocomplete.
- KeyboardKit Pro unlocks localized callouts for all locales.
- KeyboardKit Pro can perform dictation from the keyboard.
- KeyboardKit Pro unlocks a powerful emoji keyboard.
- KeyboardKit Pro can detect if an external keyboard is connected.
- KeyboardKit Pro can identify specific host applications.
- KeyboardKit Pro unlocks localized layouts for all locales.
- Services & views for all locales.
- KeyboardKit Pro unlocks keyboard and theme previews.
- KeyboardKit Pro unlocks ways for
to read the full document.
- KeyboardKit Pro unlocks tools to let you type within the keyboard.
- KeyboardKit Pro unlocks a theme engine with many pre-defined themes.
The demo app in the [open-source repository][SDK] lets you try out both KeyboardKit & KeyboardKit Pro. The
on the App Store lets you try many pro features directly on your iPhone & iPad, without having to write any code.
is a commercial, closed-source SDK that requires a license to be used.

abstract.text: KeyboardKit helps you create custom keyboard extensions with Swift and SwiftUI.
This protocol is used to let device-based services share
behavior, when basing a layout on different device types.
This namespace contains essential keyboard-related types.
This protocol is implemented by locale-specific services.
This class has observable states and persistent settings.
This namespace contains gesture-related types.
This protocol can be implemented by any classes that can
be used to control a keyboard extension.
This article describes the KeyboardKit gesture engine.
This article describes the KeyboardKit dictation engine.
This protocol can be implemented by any type that can be
used to trigger audio and haptic feedback.
This namespace defines preview-related type variants for
many types and protocols.
This article describes the KeyboardKit navigation engine.
This enum defines all supported Emoji versions, that are
currently available to macOS.
This button can be used to trigger gesture-based actions.
This is the main input controller in a KeyboardKit-based
keyboard extension.
This type defines the full set of keys on a keyboard and
is also a namespace for layout-related types.
This protocol can be implemented by any classes that can
generate keyboard layouts in various ways.
This namespace contains dictation-related types.
This namespace contains integration-related types.
This class has observable, keyboard status-related state.
This article describes the KeyboardKit action engine.
This namespace contains keyboard callout-related types.
This struct defines a keyboard-specific theme, which can
be used to define a bunch of styles at once.
This context has observable callout-related state and is
used for both input and action callouts.
This protocol can be implemented by any classes that can
perform dictation from a keyboard extension or in an app.
This guide describes how to get started with KeyboardKit.
Project version string for KeyboardKitPro.

make it possible to create mutable copies.

This protocol can be implemented by any classes that can
handle triggered
events.
This protocol can be implemented by any classes that can
perform dictation by accessing the microphone.

using a

perform speech recognition.

This type can be used to define important properties for
your app, and is also a namespace for app-based features.
This protocol can be implemented by theme-specific style
variations, to ensure that a style creates unique copies.
This view can be used to mimic a native iOS keyboard.
This protocol is an alias for other protocols that model
types in the framework should conform to.
This view can be used to type within keyboard extensions.
This struct represents a software license and also works
as a namespace for license-related types.
This protocol can be implemented by any type that should
be used as a license-based feature.
This article describes the KeyboardKit emoji engine.
This protocol can be implemented by any type that can be
used to open URLs from anywhere.
This article describes the KeyboardKit device utilities.
This namespace contains proxy-related types.

provide dynamic keyboard styles.

This article describes the KeyboardKit preview engine.
This namespace contains keyboard feedback-related types.
This namespace contains style-related types.
This article describes the KeyboardKit styling engine.

as their emojis.
This enum defines keyboard-specific actions, and is also
a namespace for keyboard action-related types.

with

used to perform keyboard-related callout actions.
This article describes the KeyboardKit status engine.
This enum defines supported interface orientations.
